tagged_story_for_tts:
Flat paper. Real mountains.

"Map" comes from the Latin mappa—a cloth napkin used to sketch land. Like tracing a dragon’s scales onto parchment: you capture the shape, not the fire.

A mountain isn’t a triangle. But on a map, it becomes one. Contour lines—those wavy rings—are terrain’s shorthand, each loop marking a fixed height (say, every 10 meters). Stack them close? Steep cliff. Spread apart? Gentle slope. It’s like slicing a cake horizontally: the layers show its shape without drawing every crumb.

Satellites and lasers now measure elevation to centimeters, yet even digital maps simplify. Google Earth’s 3D hills are just flat images draped over a wireframe mesh—like wrapping gift paper around a cardboard box. The illusion works until you zoom too close.

Three related subjects are terrain shadow tricks, how GPS flattens Earth, and why old maps lied.
